narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 43% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction draws from your academic record, including a strong start in your first year where you ranked in the top decile of your school, but a dip to the fourth decile by your third year, alongside your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and a notably low LSAT score of 21.50. You're from a middle-income family, identify as an Asian male planning to study full-time, which is common among applicants. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your third-year school decile ranking, as it strongly pointed toward a higher risk of bar failure compared to typical students who maintain steadier performance. Next in influence was your first-year decile, which unexpectedly nudged the prediction toward failure despite being above average, followed closely by your family income bracket. Your racial background had a similar pushing effect toward the unfavorable outcome, then your LSAT score reinforced that risk, with your undergraduate GPA also contributing in the same direction. In contrast, your gender as male slightly favored a passing prediction, while your full-time status had a minor pull toward failure. Overall, these factors placed you below the profile of students who typically pass the bar, especially since your later academic decile and test scores lag behind dataset averages like the usual LSAT around 37 or third-year deciles near 5.5. To boost your future chances, focus on rebuilding momentum in advanced coursework or similar metrics if reapplying, as early strengths like your first-year performance show potential. Consider targeted test prep to lift scores like your LSAT on a retake, since low standardized testing was a key drag here. Strengthening your GPA through post-undergrad achievements or bar-specific study habits could also shift the balance. With dedication to these areas, you could align more closely with successful profiles and improve your bar passage odds significantly. Keep pushing—you've already shown you can excel early on.
